# [policy-templates](https://mozilla.github.io/policy-templates/)

> \[!WARNING\] Documentation for policy behavior and syntax is being migrated to the [Firefox administrator reference](https://firefox-admin-docs.mozilla.org/).

Firefox policies can be specified using the [Group Policy templates on Windows](https://github.com/mozilla/policy-templates/tree/master/windows), [Intune on Windows](https://support.mozilla.org/kb/managing-firefox-intune), [configuration profiles on macOS](https://github.com/mozilla/policy-templates/tree/master/mac), or by creating a file called `policies.json`. On Windows, create a directory called `distribution` where the EXE is located and place the file there. On Mac, the file goes into `Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution`. On Linux, the file goes into `firefox/distribution`, where `firefox` is the installation directory for firefox, which varies by distribution or you can specify system-wide policy by placing the file in `/etc/firefox/policies`.

Unfortunately, JSON files do not support comments, but you can add extra entries to the JSON to use as comments. You will see an error in about:policies, but the policies will still work properly. For example:

```
{
  "policies": {
    "Authentication": {
      "SPNEGO": ["mydomain.com", "https://myotherdomain.com"]
    }
    "Authentication_Comment": "These domains are required for us"
  }
}
```

Note: The `policies.json` must use the UTF-8 encoding.

- Dotfiles-owned settings will sync cross-device: `.granted/config`, `.granted/firefox-profiles`, and the proposed `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json` would live in git and be applied by `setup.sh` on each device.
- Firefox UI changes will not automatically sync through dotfiles: if you change a Firefox preference manually in `about:config`, install extensions manually, or tweak profile state, dotfiles will not capture that unless we explicitly manage those files.
- Firefox Sync can sync some browser data separately: bookmarks, history, passwords, extensions, some settings, tabs, etc. It does not sync our enterprise `policies.json`.

Best split:

1. Use dotfiles for deterministic setup:
   - Firefox installed.
   - Granted extension/policies configured.
   - Containers enabled.
   - Granted AWS profile mappings synced.

2. Use Firefox Sync for personal browser state:
   - Bookmarks.
   - History.
   - Passwords if desired.
   - Extensions/settings supported by Firefox Sync.

3. Avoid stowing a full Firefox profile unless you explicitly want dotfiles to own browser profile state. That would sync more, but risks clobbering cookies, sessions, local profile data, and machine-specific state.

If you want custom Firefox prefs to be cross-device via dotfiles, I’d add a repo-managed `user.js` template plus a setup helper that applies it to the active/default Firefox profile. Best approach: sync only a repo-managed `user.js`, not the whole Firefox profile.

That gives you:

- Synced `about:config` prefs across devices.
- No ownership of cookies, history, sessions, logins, cache, or profile databases.
- Repeatable setup through dotfiles.
- Safe per-device Firefox profiles.

How it would work:

1. Keep canonical prefs in dotfiles, e.g. `scripts/setup/firefox/user.js`.

2. Add a setup helper that finds the active Firefox profile from:

   `~/Library/Application Support/Firefox/profiles.ini`

3. The helper installs only this file into the profile:

   `~/Library/Application Support/Firefox/Profiles/<profile>/user.js`

4. It does not touch:
   - `cookies.sqlite`
   - `places.sqlite`
   - `logins.json`
   - `key4.db`
   - `sessionstore*`
   - `prefs.js`

Important behavior: Firefox applies `user.js` on startup and writes those prefs into `prefs.js`. So this is not just initial setup; it enforces those selected prefs each time Firefox launches. That is usually what you want for dotfiles-managed `about:config`.

I’d update the plan to use:

- `policies.json` for machine/browser-level setup: containers, Granted extension, telemetry defaults.
- `user.js` for your personal `about:config` preferences.
- Firefox Sync for browser data like bookmarks/history/extensions if you want that.
- No stowed full profile, no `profiles.ini` ownership.

One decision before implementation: should the setup helper apply `user.js` to only the default Firefox profile, or to all local Firefox profiles?

My recommendation: default profile only, with an optional flag later...

For one-time initial settings, using `prefs.js` mutations with a script can be risky because Firefox may overwrite those changes, so it's not generally recommended. On the other hand, enterprise setups can use `policies.json` to lock or set default preferences. With the `Preferences` policy, I can set preferences using "default" or "locked" statuses. # [policy-templates](https://mozilla.github.io/policy-templates/)

> \[!WARNING\] Documentation for policy behavior and syntax is being migrated to the [Firefox administrator reference](https://firefox-admin-docs.mozilla.org/).

Firefox policies can be specified using the [Group Policy templates on Windows](https://github.com/mozilla/policy-templates/tree/master/windows), [Intune on Windows](https://support.mozilla.org/kb/managing-firefox-intune), [configuration profiles on macOS](https://github.com/mozilla/policy-templates/tree/master/mac), or by creating a file called `policies.json`. On Windows, create a directory called `distribution` where the EXE is located and place the file there. On Mac, the file goes into `Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution`. On Linux, the file goes into `firefox/distribution`, where `firefox` is the installation directory for firefox, which varies by distribution or you can specify system-wide policy by placing the file in `/etc/firefox/policies`.

Unfortunately, JSON files do not support comments, but you can add extra entries to the JSON to use as comments. You will see an error in about:policies, but the policies will still work properly. For example:

```
{
  "policies": {
    "Authentication": {
      "SPNEGO": ["mydomain.com", "https://myotherdomain.com"]
    }
    "Authentication_Comment": "These domains are required for us"
  }
}
```

Note: The `policies.json` must use the UTF-8 encoding.

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # Filtered Test Runner - Run specific test groups from dotfiles test suites
3: # Usage: ./scripts/test-filter.sh [group] [--list]
4: #
5: # This is a "harness engineering" tool designed for AI agents.
6: # Instead of running full test suites, agents can run targeted test groups
7: # to get faster feedback on specific changes.
8: #
9: # Examples:
10: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh fish          # Test Fish shell config only
11: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh stow          # Test stow compatibility only
12: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh claude        # Test Claude Code config only
13: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax  # Validate setup.sh syntax only
14: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile      # Validate Brewfile structure
15: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh mcp           # Test MCP server parity
16: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh all           # Run all groups
17: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh --list        # List available test groups
18: 
19: set -euo pipefail
20: 
21: # Colors
22: RED='\033[0;31m'
23: GREEN='\033[0;32m'
24: YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
25: BLUE='\033[0;34m'
26: NC='\033[0m'
27: 
28: SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
29: DOTFILES_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
30: 
31: TESTS_RUN=0
32: TESTS_PASSED=0
33: TESTS_FAILED=0
34: 
35: run_test() {
36:     local test_name="$1"
37:     local test_command="$2"
38:     TESTS_RUN=$((TESTS_RUN + 1))
39:     if eval "$test_com...

1: # Granted Configuration Guide
2: 
3: This directory contains configuration for [Granted](https://granted.dev), an AWS credential management tool with autocomplete, browser integration, and Firefox container colors/icons.
4: 
5: ## What You Should See
6: 
7: ### Terminal Autocomplete
8: 
9: When you type `assume` followed by space and Tab:
10: 
11: ```bash
12: assume <TAB>
13: ```
14: 
15: Expected: AWS profile names from `~/.aws/config`.
16: 
17: Not expected: Granted command options or local file names.
18: 
19: ### Firefox Browser Integration
20: 
21: When you open an AWS console through Granted:
22: 
23: - Firefox opens instead of Chrome or Safari.
24: - Granted opens the console in a Firefox container tab.
25: - Container colors/icons come from `.granted/firefox-profiles`.
26: 
27: Examples:
28: 
29: ```text
30: production=red:briefcase
31: development=green:tree
32: staging=yellow:cart
33: security=purple:fingerprint
34: ```
35: 
36: ## Setup
37: 
38: ### Automatic Setup
39: 
40: The dotfiles setup script handles the reproducible pieces:
41: 
42: ```bash
43: ./scripts/setup.sh
44: ```
45: 
46: Setup does the following:
47: 
48: - Installs Firefox from `homebrew/Brewfile` and `phase_9_fonts_and_apps`.
49: - Uses stow to sync `.granted/config` and `.granted/firefox-profiles` into `~/.granted`.
50: - Applies `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json` to the Firefox app bundle when writable.
51: - Installs `scripts/setup/firefox/user.js` into th...

## High

1. **Firefox auto-update wipes the enterprise policy silently.**
   `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh:193` writes into `Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution/policies.json`. Any subsequent `brew upgrade --cask firefox` (or in‑app update) replaces the bundle and deletes `policies.json`, which means the Granted extension force‑install and the telemetry/Pocket/first‑run lockdowns silently disappear until `setup.sh` is re-run. There's no drift detector or reinstall hook. Either move the policy to `/Library/Application Support/Mozilla/distribution/policies.json` (system-wide, survives upgrades; needs sudo) or wire a `brew bundle`/upgrade hook to re-invoke `firefox-setup.sh`.

2. **`install_user_js` only seeds the *default* profile, but Granted opens whichever profile is current.**
   `firefox-setup.sh:208-222` resolves a single profile via `find_default_profile`. Any user who has multiple Firefox profiles (e.g. a personal profile and a work profile selected at launch) will get container support on only one of them. `privacy.userContext.enabled=true` is required for Granted's container colour/icon mapping to work; in the unconfigured profile, `assume -c prod` will open in the default container with no isolation. Either enumerate every profile listed in `profiles.ini` and write `user.js` into each, or m...

1: # Granted Configuration Guide
2: 
3: This directory contains configuration for [Granted](https://granted.dev), an AWS credential management tool with autocomplete, browser integration, and Firefox container colors/icons.
4: 
5: ## What You Should See
6: 
7: ### Terminal Autocomplete
8: 
9: When you type `assume` followed by space and Tab:
10: 
11: ```bash
12: assume <TAB>
13: ```
14: 
15: Expected: AWS profile names from `~/.aws/config`.
16: 
17: Not expected: Granted command options or local file names.
18: 
19: ### Firefox Browser Integration
20: 
21: When you open an AWS console through Granted:
22: 
23: - Firefox opens instead of Chrome or Safari.
24: - Granted opens the console in a Firefox container tab.
25: - Container colors/icons come from `.granted/firefox-profiles`.
26: 
27: Examples:
28: 
29: ```text
30: production=red:briefcase
31: development=green:tree
32: staging=yellow:cart
33: security=purple:fingerprint
34: ```
35: 
36: ## Setup
37: 
38: ### Automatic Setup
39: 
40: The dotfiles setup script handles the reproducible pieces:
41: 
42: ```bash
43: ./scripts/setup.sh
44: ```
45: 
46: Setup does the following:
47: 
48: - Installs Firefox from `homebrew/Brewfile` and `phase_9_fonts_and_apps`.
49: - Uses stow to sync `.granted/config` and `.granted/firefox-profiles` into `~/.granted`.
50: - Applies `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json` to the Firefox app bundle when writable.
51: - Installs `scripts/setup/fi...

parts.text:
**Verifying directory paths**

I’m considering that using grep might be too broad and noisy for this purpose. Instead, I have a useful review hinting at a system-wide path, specifically /Library/Application Support/Mozilla/distribution. I’m wondering if I need to verify that. The web documentation didn’t mention it, but it could still be useful. Maybe I should check if that directory exists using the 'ls' command? Do I even need to do a file search?

Mozilla’s current policy docs only document the app-bundle `policies.json` path on macOS. I’m not going to switch to an undocumented path; instead I’ll make the setup helper report the admin install command clearly and keep `user.js` enforced without admin rights.

**Improving installation policy**

I’m considering whether to improve the installation policy to make sudo optional. The current warning may not be sufficient, especially if users don’t have admin access. I wonder if we could utilize a privileged prompt or introduce an environment variable, like FIREFOX_POLICY_USE_SUDO? The challenge is that automatic setups won't work without sudo. If a command is not writable but sudo exists and the terminal is interactive, maybe we can prompt for it. I need to ensure future devices can prompt interactively too.

To make this more automatic on real device setup runs, I’m adding an interactive sudo fallback: if Firefox’s app bundle is not writable and setup is running in a terminal, it will prompt for admin rights and install the policy. Non-interactive CI/agent runs will still skip safely.

content.summary:
User quoted the earlier recommendation: “Auto-discard only after a tab has been inactive for a while, e.g. 30-60 minutes.” Then asked: “If you discard does it delete the tab entirely or can i just refresh it and bring it back?” Assistant answered that discarding does not delete the tab: it keeps the visible tab, preserves URL/history/title/favicon usually, frees memory by killing/unloading the page process, and reloads/restores when clicked. Caveat: live page memory can be lost (unsaved forms, drafts, in-memory app state, some logged-in views). Safe for docs/articles/GitHub/search results; pin/allowlist draft editors, dashboards, chats, video calls, live tools.

User then requested: “Implement this change so it automatically applies from ~/dotfiles to all devices”. Work was performed in `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles` to make Firefox tab discarding and low-memory unload behavior dotfiles-managed and setup-applied.

Context/findings before edits:
- Existing Firefox setup system already exists and is invoked by `scripts/setup.sh` around lines 2027-2029. `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh` manages Firefox enterprise policy and default-profile `user.js`, plus `userChrome.css`, `userContent.css`, and Sidebery CSS.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh` has browser tests covering Firefox setup/policy/user.js. `homebrew/Brewfile` already includes Firefox cask support from prior Firefox setup work.
- `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json` initially had policies for telemetry/default browser/default bookmarks, force-installed Granted extension `{b5e0e8de-ebfe-4306-9528-bcc18241a490}`, and default prefs for containers and dark theme.
- `scripts/setup/firefox/user.js` already had dotfiles-managed prefs, including containers, dark theme, userChrome CSS support, startup/new-tab noise reduction, telemetry/Pocket, and captured prefs block.
- `ocv attach --help`/OpenCode context from prior compressed work is no longer directly needed for this task.

Web/API verification:
- Fetched AMO API for `auto-tab-discard`; package URL confirmed via `https://addons.mozilla.org/firefox/downloads/latest/auto-tab-discard/latest.xpi`.
- Downloaded XPI into `/var/folders/rm/hrftyllx1jz3ty2b71g2528h0000gp/T/opencode/firefox-auto-tab-discard/auto-tab-discard.xpi` and extracted `manifest.json` with `unzip -p ... manifest.json | python3 -m json.tool`.
- Manifest details: extension name `Auto Tab Discard`, version `0.6.7`, Gecko ID `{c2c003ee-bd69-42a2-b0e9-6f34222cb046}`, managed storage schema present at `schema.json`.
- Extracted `schema.json`; relevant managed policy keys include `period` (seconds), `number` (tabs before trigger), `audio`, `pinned`, `form`, `battery`, `online`, `mode`, `memory-enabled`, `simultaneous-jobs`, `whitelist`, `whitelist-url`, `trash.enabled`, `startup-unpinned`, `startup-pinned`, etc.
- Mozilla policy docs were fetched. Docs state on macOS `policies.json` goes into `Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution`. No documented per-user macOS policies path was confirmed. Therefore implementation stays with app-bundle policy install plus sudo fallback, not an undocumented `/Library` path.

Files edited:
- `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json`:
  - Added force-install entry for Auto Tab Discard extension ID `{c2c003ee-bd69-42a2-b0e9-6f34222cb046}` with `install_url`: `https://addons.mozilla.org/firefox/downloads/latest/auto-tab-discard/latest.xpi`.
  - Added `3rdparty.Extensions.{c2c003ee-bd69-42a2-b0e9-6f34222cb046}` managed policy:
    - `period`: `3600` (60 minutes inactive before discard)
    - `number`: `8` (only triggers once enough tabs exist)
    - `audio`: `true` (do not discard tabs playing audio)
    - `pinned`: `true` (do not discard pinned tabs)
    - `form`: `true` (do not discard tabs with changed form data)
    - `online`: `true` (do not discard if there is no internet connection)
    - `mode`: `time-based`
    - `memory-enabled`: `false`
    - `simultaneous-jobs`: `1`
    - `trash.enabled`: `false`
    - `startup-unpinned`: `false`
    - `startup-pinned`: `false`
    - `whitelist-url`: `*://github.com/*`, `*://*.github.com/*`, `*://*.atlassian.net/*`, `*://app.slack.com/*`, `*://teams.microsoft.com/*`, `*://meet.google.com/*`, `*://*.zoom.us/*`.
  - Added preference `browser.tabs.unloadOnLowMemory` with `{ "Value": true, "Status": "default" }`.
- `scripts/setup/firefox/user.js`:
  - Added comment: “Prefer unloading idle/background tabs over swapping the whole browser under memory pressure. Auto Tab Discard's 60-minute policy is managed by scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json.”
  - Added `user_pref("browser.tabs.unloadOnLowMemory", true);`.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh`:
  - Added browser tests:
    - `Firefox policy installs Auto Tab Discard` asserts ExtensionSettings entry for `{c2c003ee-bd69-42a2-b0e9-6f34222cb046}`, force_installed, and URL contains `auto-tab-discard/latest.xpi`.
    - `Firefox policy configures tab discard` asserts `period == 3600`, `pinned/audio/form` true, and whitelist contains `*.atlassian.net/*`.
    - `Firefox policy enables low-memory tab unload` asserts `browser.tabs.unloadOnLowMemory` policy value true.
    - `Firefox user.js enables low-memory tab unload` greps for `browser.tabs.unloadOnLowMemory.*, true`.
- `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh`:
  - Added `install_policy_with_sudo(policy_dir, policy_target)` helper:
    - Returns failure if `FIREFOX_POLICY_USE_SUDO` is not `1` (defaults to `1`), stdin is not a TTY, or `sudo` unavailable.
    - Logs that admin rights are required and runs `sudo mkdir -p "$policy_dir" && sudo cp "$POLICY_SOURCE" "$policy_target" && sudo chmod 0644 "$policy_target"`.
  - Updated `install_policy()` so when `$policy_dir` exists but is not writable, or when `$policy_dir` missing and Firefox resources dir is not writable, it first tries `install_policy_with_sudo`; on success logs `Installed Firefox policies.json with sudo` and returns `0`; otherwise prints the manual sudo command and skips. This keeps non-interactive CI/agent runs safe but allows real device setup to prompt for admin.

Validation already run:
- `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`: passed 10/10.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`: passed 2/2.
- Accidentally ran `scripts/test-filter.sh setup`; it is not a valid group. Output listed available groups and indicated Firefox checks live in `browser`.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh browser`: passed 68/68 after policy/user.js/test additions.
- Ran `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh` before sudo fallback was added. Result:
  - Could not create `/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution` (no admin/non-sudo at that time), printed manual policy install warning.
  - Successfully backed up existing profile `user.js` to `/Users/shahe.islam/Library/Application Support/Firefox/Profiles/ewb7hoy2.default-release/user.js.backup.20260602-193716`.
  - Installed Firefox default-profile `user.js`.
  - `userChrome.css`, `sidebery.css`, and `userContent.css` were already current.
  - Completed with warnings.
- No validation has been rerun after adding `install_policy_with_sudo`; should run at least `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax` and `scripts/test-filter.sh browser` again.

Current active state before user interruption:
- Dotfiles source includes the tab-discard policy and tests.
- Current device has profile-level `user.js` applied, but app-bundle enterprise `policies.json` was not installed yet because previous run lacked sudo fallback/admin. Therefore Auto Tab Discard force-install/managed settings are not active on this device until `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh` is rerun with sudo available or manual sudo copy is performed.
- Assistant was about to continue by rerunning validation and then installing policy with sudo.

User then said via system reminder: “You should be able to run sudo permissions”. Operational mode changed from plan to build. Next action should acknowledge and proceed: rerun validation, then rerun `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh` in an interactive/sudo-capable way. If `sudo` prompts and tool cannot supply password, report that manual admin prompt/password entry is required; otherwise verify `/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution/policies.json` exists and matches source. Firefox must be restarted for policy/extension to load; check `about:policies` after restart if desired.

**Finalizing setup command**

I need to ensure I'm providing a clear command for the user to run. The message shouldn't include "copy/save," but the command itself is fine. I also want to note that Firefox will need to be restarted, and that tests have passed. The setup helper will install on devices where the command is run from the terminal, as nonTTY is preventing it. It seems `osascript` failed due to sandbox restrictions. Maybe we don't actually need to modify anything after all.

Implemented and validated the Firefox tab discard setup in dotfiles.

What changed:
- `scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json`
  - Force-installs Auto Tab Discard.
  - Configures 60-minute inactive tab discard.
  - Preserves pinned tabs, audio tabs, tabs with changed forms, and key allowlisted sites.
  - Enables `browser.tabs.unloadOnLowMemory`.
- `scripts/setup/firefox/user.js`
  - Enables Firefox low-memory tab unloading.
- `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh`
  - Adds sudo-aware policy installation for normal device setup.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh`
  - Adds tests for the new Firefox policy and user.js settings.

Validation:
- `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`: passed `10/10`
- `scripts/test-filter.sh browser`: passed `68/68`

Device apply status:
- Your profile `user.js` is already current.
- The Firefox enterprise policy is not installed on this Mac yet because this tool session cannot complete sudo writes to `/Applications/Firefox.app/...` non-interactively. Direct `sudo` failed with “terminal is required”, and macOS admin prompt install failed with `Operation not permitted`.

Run this in your terminal to activate the policy on this device:

```bash
sudo mkdir -p "/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution" && sudo cp "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/setup/firefox/policies.json" "/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution/policies.json" && sudo chmod 0644 "/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/Resources/distribution/policies.json"
```

Then restart Firefox. Check `about:policies` to confirm Auto Tab Discard and the managed preferences are active.
